subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that smp_wmb() may not be sufficient for ordering and suggested using a dsb(ishst) instruction, as used in the GIC IPI code, to ensure proper ordering.
reviews.raw_body:
The smp_wmb() is not sufficient here. In the GIC IPI code we use a
dsb(ishst). It should be similar here. I think TF-A does this already
but it's unclear from the SDEI spec that it is mandated.

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: Author agreed to address two issues: adding a barrier in the backtrace path of patch 3/4 and updating the comment in that same patch, matching the approach used in gic_ipi_send_mask().
reviews.raw_body:
You're right, I'll switch it to dsb(ishst) to match gic_ipi_send_mask().

And as you pointed, the xame fix is needed in 3/4: the backtrace path
publishes backtrace_mask before signalling and currently has no barrier
at all (as the bot spotted), so it needs the dsb(ishst) there too.

I'll also drop the now-incorrect "cumulative wmb" comment.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.
